sections.heading: Intelligent Agent
sections.body: An intelligent agent is anything that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon that environment through actuators. This is a deliberately general definition — it covers everything from a thermostat to a self-driving car to a software chatbot.

sections.heading: Rational Agent
sections.body: A rational agent is one that, for each possible sequence of perceptions, selects the action expected to maximize its performance measure — given the evidence provided by its percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge it has. Rationality is about making the best decision given available information, not about achieving a perfect outcome every time.

sections.heading: Agent Function
sections.body: The agent function is an abstract mathematical description mapping every possible percept sequence the agent could receive to an action. It describes the agent's behavior completely, in principle, though it may be far too large to write out explicitly for any complex agent.

sections.heading: Agent Program
sections.body: The agent program is the concrete implementation that runs on the agent's actual hardware/architecture and realizes the agent function in practice — it's the real, runnable code, as opposed to the agent function's abstract mathematical description.

sections.heading: Types of Agents
sections.body: Agents are commonly classified by how sophisticated their decision process is: simple reflex agents (react to current percept only), model-based agents (maintain internal state), goal-based agents (act to achieve explicit goals), utility-based agents (weigh outcomes by preference), and learning agents (improve over time from experience).

sections.heading: Agent Environment
sections.body: The environment is everything external to the agent that it perceives and acts upon. Environments can be characterized along several dimensions: fully vs. partially observable, deterministic vs. stochastic, episodic vs. sequential, static vs. dynamic, discrete vs. continuous, and single-agent vs. multi-agent.

sections.heading: PEAS Representation
sections.body: PEAS is a framework for specifying a task environment precisely: Performance measure (how success is judged), Environment (the world the agent operates in), Actuators (how it acts), and Sensors (how it perceives). For example, a self-driving taxi's PEAS might be: performance = safe/fast/legal/comfortable trip; environment = roads, traffic, pedestrians; actuators = steering/accelerator/brake/signal; sensors = cameras/GPS/speedometer.

quiz.question: What defines an intelligent agent?
options: Something that only stores data with no actions
Anything that perceives its environment via sensors and acts via actuators

quiz.question: A rational agent chooses actions to:
options: Maximize its expected performance measure given available evidence
Minimize the number of sensors used

quiz.question: In the PEAS framework, what does the "E" stand for?
